questions.questionText: A 40-year-old man has episodic vertigo lasting hours, low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness. Diagnosis?
questions.optionB: Meniere's disease (endolymphatic hydrops — triad/tetrad: episodic vertigo + fluctuating SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness)
questions.explanation: Core concept: Meniere's = endolymphatic hydrops → episodic vertigo + fluctuating low-frequency SNHL + tinnitus + aural fullness. Why correct: Classic tetrad. Episodes last 20 minutes to hours. Initially unilateral. Low-frequency SNHL initially (progresses to all frequencies). Why A wrong: BPPV — brief (<1 minute) positional vertigo, canalith repositioning (Epley maneuver), NO hearing loss. Why C wrong: Vestibular neuritis — single episode of severe prolonged vertigo, NO hearing loss. Why D wrong: Acoustic neuroma — progressive unilateral SNHL, tinnitus, NO episodic vertigo. Clinical relevance: Treatment: salt restriction, diuretics (hydrochlorothiazide), betahistine. Intractable: intratympanic gentamicin, endolymphatic sac surgery, vestibular nerve section. Exam trap: BPPV = seconds/minutes, positional. Meniere's = hours. Vestibular neuritis = days. Duration differentiates. Memory aid: 'Meniere's = Minutes-to-Hours of vertigo + Hearing loss + Tinnitus + fullness.' Reference: Dhingra – Inner Ear.
questions.conceptTag: Meniere disease endolymphatic hydrops vertigo SNHL
